name: Study 1: A Bad Plan to Convert an Advantage

comments.text:
Welcome to the first study of the Queen Endgames chapter!

In this study, we'll take a look at a game between two 2100 rated opponents online, one of which is a Gotham Sub.

Both had a queen and a minor piece, while White had a majority on the kingside.

Black had a 2 on 1 on the queenside.

White gets the e5-square for the knight and attacks the queen on e7.

This is crucial, as the knight can jump to f7 or g6 later and attack the king.
And now White can centralize their own queen, defend the knight and attack the b7-pawn.
Sliding to a4.

But White played Kf5, with the idea of supporting the f-pawn and simply pushing to victory.

Black resigned here.

Back to a5.

Will we see a repetition?
The queen has to retreat.

And White forced a trade of queens!

They calculated the pawn race until the end.
Black could give a check here...
Black had the opportunity to push it, though.
White didn't mind giving checks, as they were the one fighting for a draw.
White still had the king.
Repeating the position once.
Staying on the back rank.

White played Kg2 here, escaping promotion with check and reintroducing the possibility of Qa8+.

But a simple Qa3+, winning the a2-pawn, was sufficient.

Black started moving towards the queenside.

In queen endgames, the king must help out, despite enduring a lot of checks!
Going forward with the king.
Centralizing the queen and giving a check on e4.
White can force a draw.
Black blocked with a pawn...
And Black captured the knight with check.
Still pushing, now protected by the queen.
But Black can still take it!
The only square for the king.
They tried keeping the king close...
Black captured, naturally.
Another check.

White played the best move here.

Pushing to g5, protected by the queen, was the way to go.

Now they would trade on g5 and get two connected passed pawns themselves.
A check from the f3-square now.
The only way Black could escape repetition was to play ...Kf7.
Escaping to b6.
Instead of going back to b6, Black chose the a6-square.

White had to create some sort of counterplay.

So they started pushing on the kingside.
Once Black gets the bishop back, hoping to push the two connected passed pawns on the queenside...
A check in the center.
Check.
White gave a check from c3 again.
The king went back to c5 now.

Black slid to d7 now. 

However, White was keeping the checks going, and it wasn't clear how Black was going to move forward!

Black's two pawns were very dangerous.

So White's best bet was giving checks.
Giving a check on b1 was stronger.

Now Black can start pushing with ...a5.

This was the fastest way forward.
Black tried pushing...
The g8-square is more sensible, not to walk into a pin.

Another check.

White was still content with a draw.
White blocked the pawns right away...
But White was up a whole queen.

Back to e6.

Repeating moves?

And Black threw the game away!

Probably in time trouble, they thought that after promotion White had Qa8+.

But Black would have promoted with check!
Black disallowed f7, though.
They simply kept pushing.

Now the king went to a6.

No more checks!
A check by White.
Here comes perpetual check.
Escaping to g1.

Another check from the c3-square.

White didn't want to let Black's king come closer!

But once the queen was on the b2-square, there was no more hope for Black.

They would soon run out of moves.

Although counterintuitive, White should push the g-pawn in front of the king here.

The idea is to get the king into the game via the g2-square.
White promoted first...

White started off with a check to the king.

The queen was now defending the knight at the same time.
Now that the king was supporting the queenside pawns, they started pushing as well.
Only move.
The king went after the f-pawn.
There weren't many options for Black here.
One square away for Black.
Back to b6.
Getting the king closer.
Only move.
When White escapes to h2...
Sliding to g8.
They went back to d7, patrolling the seventh rank and attacking the c7-pawn.

But had to side step as a queen trade was threatened.

Of course, White can't capture and leave themselves in a king endgame with both sides of the board to defend.
A good move by White, restricting Black from moving around.
But White's queen simply stopped promotion on a1.
The king was still closer to the queenside, though.

Both players trying to go forward.

But Black is closer!

For some reason, White didn't capture it.

Maybe it was time trouble.
Back to b4!
However, White stopped it and attacked the a6-pawn at the same time.

Black blocked with the queen.

But that left the h6-pawn hanging!

Qa2+ would run into ...Qa3.

That's why White gave the check from the c2-square.
A pawn race.

So White gave a check.

Moving the queen with tempo is always useful.
Black had to take...
And the king slid to e6.

But White had another passed pawn.

Bringing the king closer and winning Black's pawns also works.
If Black captures on a5...
The king still had to go through some checks, though.
Realizing they were lost now, Black tried bringing the king closer.

And Black decided to go back and defend the pawn on b7.

Of course, their plan consisted of pushing the queenside pawns for the most part.

White immediately made a mistake!

They wanted to defend their a5-pawn.
Here's another one, disallowing the king from going to c4.

Black kept their queen near the center, defending the a6-pawn.

A mistake, though, which allowed White more checks.
Black finally got the chance to start pushing, too.

Blocking again.

Now the h6-pawn couldn't be captured.

If Black could evade the checks and push the pawns, it would be great for them.
The safest square.

But now the queen blocked!

Again, White mustn't trade queens.
Back to d3, with check.
Another check by White.
The brave king started running up the board!

And now the f6-pawn couldn't be stopped.

But the a4-pawn couldn't be stopped as well!

White chose not to and captured the g7-pawn!

Now the game was not so clear, as White had the passed f-pawn.

Instead of going for ...b4, however, Black got their queen to g6, attacking the g4-pawn.

They probably wanted to make sure there was no danger before they continued pushing.
White was a square closer.
But stepping into checking grounds!
The king went to b4.
And White recaptured.
White chose to capture the bishop, which is even worse than moving the queen to c4.

Now Black played ...Kc5.

White just had to be careful for a moment.

Capturing on b3 would allow promotion on a1.
The checks were still possible, though.
Another check by White.

Only move.

And the game would end in a draw.

Of course, this is only one of the lines that could arise, but shows that giving up the a5-pawn is okay as long as the knight gets the e5-square!

It's all about activity.
White can simply escape to g1 and g2.
